message.content:
Adversaries may exploit systemd timers to schedule the execution of malicious code in Linux environments. Systemd timers, which are unit files with a .timer extension, control services and can be set to run based on calendar events or time spans. These timers serve as an alternative to Cron and can be activated remotely via the systemctl command over SSH. Each .timer file requires a corresponding .service file, which is managed by the systemd system and service manager. Privileged timers are stored in /etc/systemd/system/ and /usr/lib/systemd/system, while user-level timers are in ~/.config/systemd/user/. By using systemd timers, adversaries can achieve persistence by scheduling malicious code to run at system startup or on a recurring basis. Privileged timers can maintain root-level persistence, whereas user-level timers can ensure user-level persistence.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and auditing the creation and modification of .timer and .service files, restricting access to systemd directories, and employing file integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes. Additionally, regular reviews of scheduled tasks and services can help identify and remove malicious timers.
